subject: EVS
source: NCERT Class 5 EVS / The World Around Us PDF uploaded as v-evs.pdf
description: Teacher-style Class 5 EVS JSON notes. Every topic includes concept-based step-by-step explanation, proper important keyword definitions, examples, quick summary and revision notes.

topics.meaning: Water is a natural resource needed by all living things. Freshwater is water that is not salty and can be used for drinking, farming and daily needs.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter begins with children watching rain and wondering where water comes from and where it goes. It explains that most of Earth is covered with water, but most of it is salty ocean water. Only a very small part is freshwater, and humans, animals, birds and plants depend on it for survival, crops and daily activities.
topics.easyExplanation: Water is needed for life. We drink it, plants need it to grow, and animals also need it. But we cannot drink sea water directly because it is salty. The useful water for us is freshwater, so we must save it.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Start with the water we use every day
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Water is used for drinking, cooking, washing, cleaning, farming and for animals and plants. So the first idea is that water is connected with almost every activity of life.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Separate salty water and freshwater
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Most water on Earth is in oceans and seas, but that water is salty. We cannot drink it directly, so it is different from the freshwater we need.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Understand why freshwater is precious
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Freshwater is much less than salty water. The NCERT glass-and-teaspoon idea shows that if all water is like a glass, useful freshwater is only like a small teaspoon.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Connect freshwater with living beings
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: People, animals, birds and plants depend on freshwater. Plants need it to grow, farmers need it for crops, and animals need it to survive.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Learn the final message
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Because freshwater is limited and shared by everyone, we should not waste it and should protect sources like rivers, lakes, ponds, wells and groundwater.

examples: Using water for cooking and cleaning

importantKeywords.definition: A liquid natural resource needed by all living things for life and daily activities.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: We drink water every day.

importantKeywords.definition: Water with little or no salt, found in rain, rivers, lakes, ponds, wells and groundwater.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: River water and rainwater are freshwater sources.

importantKeywords.definition: Water that contains a lot of salt, mainly found in seas and oceans.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Ocean water is saltwater.

importantKeywords.definition: Something useful that we get from nature, such as water, air, soil, forests and sunlight.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Water, air and soil are natural resources.

importantKeywords.definition: The ability of a living being to stay alive.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Plants need water for survival.

topics.quickSummary: Freshwater is limited but essential for every living thing, so it must be protected and used wisely.
revisionNotes: Most water on Earth is salty.
Freshwater is needed for drinking, crops and daily activities.
All living things depend on water.
Freshwater should not be wasted.

topics.meaning: Sources of water are the places or forms from where we get water.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter asks students to identify freshwater sources through pictures and observation. These include rain, rivers, lakes, ponds, wells, handpumps, glaciers and groundwater. It also mentions examples like Wular Lake and traditional water storage methods such as johads.
topics.easyExplanation: We get water from many places. Rain brings water. Some water stays in rivers and ponds, some freezes as snow or glaciers, and some goes underground.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Understand what a source means
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A source of water is a place from where we get water. For example, a river is a source because people and animals can use water from it.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify visible sources
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Rivers, ponds, lakes and streams are visible freshwater sources because we can see water collected on the surface of the Earth.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify rain as a natural source
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Rain brings water from clouds to Earth. Rainwater fills rivers, ponds and lakes and also helps crops and trees.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Understand underground sources
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Some rainwater goes into the soil and becomes groundwater. Wells, handpumps, borewells and tube wells help us take this water out.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Connect different sources together
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Water sources are connected. Rain fills rivers and ponds, rivers flow across land, and some water goes underground. So saving every source is important.

importantKeywords.definition: A place from which something comes.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A river is a source of freshwater.

importantKeywords.definition: Water that falls from clouds as rain.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Rainwater fills ponds.

importantKeywords.definition: A huge mass of ice found in very cold regions.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Some rivers begin from glaciers.

importantKeywords.definition: A large body of water surrounded by land.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Wular Lake is a freshwater lake.

importantKeywords.definition: A deep hole dug in the ground to take out groundwater.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Villagers may use a well for water.

topics.quickSummary: Freshwater comes from rain, rivers, lakes, ponds, wells, handpumps, glaciers and underground storage.
revisionNotes: Rain is a major source of freshwater.
Rivers, lakes and ponds store water on the surface.
Glaciers store water as ice.
Wells and handpumps give groundwater.

topics.meaning: Forms of water are the different states in which water exists: liquid, solid and vapour.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter explains that water is seen as liquid in rain, ponds and rivers; as solid in ice and snow; and as vapour or steam when heated. Activities with ice cubes and boiling water help students observe melting, boiling and droplets forming on a cold glass.
topics.easyExplanation: Water can change its form. Ice melts into water. Water can become steam when heated. Steam can cool and become drops again.

stepByStepExplanation.title: See water as a liquid
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The water we drink, the rain that falls, and water in rivers and ponds are liquid forms of water.

stepByStepExplanation.title: See water as a solid
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: When water becomes very cold, it freezes into ice. Snow and glaciers are also solid forms of water.

stepByStepExplanation.title: See water as vapour
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: When water is heated, it changes into water vapour or steam. This vapour mixes with air and may not always be visible.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Understand change of form
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Ice melts into water when it gets heat. Water changes into vapour when it gets more heat. Vapour can cool and become droplets again.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Connect with the cold glass activity
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: When ice is placed in a steel glass, the outside becomes cold. Water vapour in the air touches the cold surface and changes into tiny droplets.

importantKeywords.definition: A form of matter that can flow and takes the shape of its container.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Water in a glass is liquid.

importantKeywords.definition: A form of matter with a fixed shape.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Ice is solid water.

importantKeywords.definition: A key term from the topic 'Forms of Water' that students should understand clearly for textbook questions and daily-life examples.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Steam is water vapour.

importantKeywords.definition: Changing from solid to liquid due to heat.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Ice melts into water.

importantKeywords.definition: Heating water until it changes rapidly into vapour.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Water boils in a kettle.

topics.quickSummary: Water exists as liquid, solid and vapour, and heating or cooling can change one form into another.
revisionNotes: Ice and snow are solid forms of water.
Rain and river water are liquid forms.
Steam is water vapour.
Heat and cooling change water forms.

topics.meaning: The water cycle is the continuous movement of water from Earth to the sky and back to Earth again.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter uses a transparent bag activity to show how sunlight heats water, water becomes vapour, droplets form inside the bag and then fall back. In nature, the Sun heats water from rivers, lakes, ponds and oceans. Vapour rises, cools, forms clouds and comes down as rain, snow or hail.
topics.easyExplanation: Water keeps moving in a circle. The Sun heats water, water goes up as vapour, clouds form, and water comes back as rain.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Sun heats water on Earth
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The Sun warms water present in oceans, rivers, lakes, ponds and wet soil.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Water changes into vapour
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Because of heat, some liquid water changes into water vapour and rises into the air. This change is called evaporation.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Vapour cools in the sky
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Higher up, the air is cooler. Water vapour cools down and changes into tiny water droplets. This is condensation.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Clouds are formed
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Many tiny droplets join together and form clouds. Clouds carry water in the sky.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Water falls back to Earth
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: When clouds become heavy, water falls as rain, snow or hail. This is precipitation.

stepByStepExplanation.title: The journey repeats
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Rainwater goes into rivers, lakes, oceans and the ground. Then the Sun heats it again, so the cycle continues.

importantKeywords.definition: The continuous movement of water from Earth to sky and back to Earth as rain, snow or hail.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Water goes up as vapour and returns as rain.

importantKeywords.definition: The process in which liquid water changes into water vapour because of heat.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Puddle water dries in sunlight.

importantKeywords.definition: The process in which water vapour cools and changes into tiny water droplets.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Drops form outside a cold glass.

importantKeywords.definition: A group of tiny water droplets or ice crystals in the sky.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Dark clouds may bring rain.

importantKeywords.definition: Water falling from clouds to Earth as rain, snow or hail.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Rainfall is precipitation.

topics.quickSummary: The water cycle explains how water moves through evaporation, condensation, cloud formation and rainfall.
revisionNotes: The Sun drives the water cycle.
Evaporation changes water into vapour.
Condensation forms droplets and clouds.
Rain, snow and hail bring water back to Earth.

topics.meaning: Groundwater is water stored under the ground in spaces between soil and rocks. Recharge means filling it again with rainwater.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter explains that when rain falls, some water is soaked by soil and sinks through layers of soil and rocks. This stored underground water is used through wells, borewells, tube wells and handpumps. It also explains that cemented surfaces stop water from entering the ground, while open areas, ponds, soak pits and trees help recharge groundwater.
topics.easyExplanation: Some rainwater goes inside the ground. This water is groundwater. We bring it out using wells and handpumps. To keep groundwater available, rainwater must be allowed to enter the soil.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Rainwater reaches the ground
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: When rain falls, some water flows on the surface, but some water touches open soil.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Soil absorbs water
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Open soil acts like a sponge. Water slowly enters the spaces between soil particles.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Water moves deeper
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The water passes through layers of soil and rocks and collects deep below the ground. This stored water is groundwater.

stepByStepExplanation.title: People take out groundwater
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Wells, handpumps, tube wells and borewells are used to draw groundwater for homes, farms and communities.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Recharge keeps groundwater available
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: When rainwater enters the ground again, groundwater is refilled. This is groundwater recharge.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Cemented surfaces create a problem
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Concrete roads and covered land stop water from entering the soil, so soak pits, ponds, open land and trees help recharge groundwater.

importantKeywords.definition: Water that seeps into the soil and is stored below the ground between soil and rocks.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Handpump water comes from groundwater.

importantKeywords.definition: The refilling of groundwater when rainwater enters the soil.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Rainwater harvesting helps recharge.

importantKeywords.definition: A deep narrow hole made to draw groundwater.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A borewell supplies water to a house.

importantKeywords.definition: A pit made in the ground to help rainwater or used water seep into the soil.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A soak pit helps recharge groundwater.

importantKeywords.definition: A hard paved surface that blocks water from entering soil.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Concrete roads reduce recharge.

topics.quickSummary: Groundwater is an important hidden water source, and recharge keeps it available for future use.
revisionNotes: Groundwater is stored under the ground.
Rainwater becomes groundwater by seeping into soil.
Wells and handpumps draw groundwater.
Open soil, trees and soak pits help recharge.
Cemented areas reduce recharge.

topics.topic: Surface Water and Flow of Rivers
topics.meaning: Surface water is water found on Earth’s surface, such as rivers, ponds, lakes and streams.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter explains that rivers, ponds and lakes are natural freshwater sources. Through an activity with mustard seeds on a folded newspaper, students learn that water flows from higher to lower places and follows the shape of land. Flowing water may stop, curve, collect or change the shape of land.
topics.easyExplanation: Surface water is water we can see on land. Rivers flow down from high places and turn according to the shape of the land.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Recognise surface water
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Water found on the surface of the Earth is surface water. Rivers, ponds, lakes and streams are examples.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Understand where rivers begin
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Many rivers begin in high places such as mountains or glaciers and then move towards lower land.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Water flows downhill
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Water naturally moves from higher places to lower places because of the slope of the land.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Land shape guides the river
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A river may bend, turn, slow down or collect depending on rocks, valleys, slopes and plains in its path.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Flowing water changes land
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: As a river flows, it can carry soil and stones, cut valleys and shape the land slowly over time.

examples: A river flowing from a hill
Rainwater running on a sloped road
A pond formed in a low area

importantKeywords.definition: Water found on the surface of the Earth, such as rivers, lakes and ponds.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A lake is surface water.

importantKeywords.definition: A large natural stream of water that flows across land, usually from high land to lower land.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: The Godavari is a river.

importantKeywords.definition: A surface that is higher at one end and lower at the other.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Water flows down a slope.

importantKeywords.definition: A natural shape of land such as a hill, valley or plain.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A mountain is a landform.

importantKeywords.definition: An area of land from which water drains into a river or lake.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Rain falling in a watershed may enter the same river.

topics.quickSummary: Surface water flows according to land shape and is an important freshwater source.
revisionNotes: Rivers, ponds and lakes are surface water.
Water flows from higher to lower ground.
Land shape controls water direction.
Flowing water can shape land.

topics.meaning: Life in water means plants and animals that live in or near water bodies. Adaptations are special features that help them survive.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter shows ponds and water bodies as habitats for fish, frogs, turtles, water snakes, birds, insects and aquatic plants. It explains that living beings have special features such as fins, long legs, floating leaves, waxy coatings and long roots to live in water.
topics.easyExplanation: Water bodies are homes. Fish swim with fins, frogs live near water, birds catch fish, and plants like lotus and water lily grow in water.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Water bodies are homes
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Ponds, lakes, rivers and wetlands are habitats for fish, frogs, turtles, insects, birds and many plants.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Animals have helpful body parts
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Fish have fins to swim, frogs have strong legs, and some birds have long legs and pointed beaks to catch food in water.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Plants have special features
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Lotus and water lily leaves float on water. Some leaves have a waxy coating that helps water roll off.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Living things depend on each other
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Small insects, plants, fish, frogs and birds are connected because many of them eat or shelter near one another.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Protect the habitat
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If water becomes dirty, aquatic plants and animals can suffer. Clean water bodies are necessary for life in water.

examples: Pond heron standing on long legs

importantKeywords.definition: Related to water; aquatic plants and animals live in water.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Lotus is an aquatic plant.

importantKeywords.definition: The natural home of a plant or animal.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A pond is a habitat for fish.

importantKeywords.definition: A special feature or habit that helps a living thing survive in its habitat.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Fish have fins for swimming.

importantKeywords.definition: Flat body parts that help fish move in water.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Fish use fins to swim.

importantKeywords.definition: A waterproof layer on some plant leaves.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Lotus leaves have a waxy coating.

topics.quickSummary: Freshwater habitats support many plants and animals with special adaptations.
revisionNotes: Water bodies are habitats.
Fish, frogs, turtles and birds depend on water.
Aquatic plants have special features.
Adaptations help living things survive.

topics.meaning: A food chain shows who eats whom in a habitat.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter uses a river food chain game where students choose roles such as small fish, big fish, frog, bird, human, crocodile or otter and connect themselves using strings. This shows that living things depend on one another for food and balance in freshwater habitats.
topics.easyExplanation: In water, one living thing may become food for another. If one animal disappears, other animals in the chain can be affected.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Start with plants and tiny organisms
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: In a pond or river, plants and tiny living things use sunlight and nutrients to grow.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Small animals eat plants or tiny organisms
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Small insects, tadpoles or tiny fish may eat plants or smaller organisms.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Bigger animals eat smaller animals
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Larger fish, frogs, turtles or birds may eat smaller fish or insects.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Understand the chain
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A food chain shows who eats whom. Energy passes from one living thing to another.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Balance is important
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If one part of the chain is harmed, other living things also get affected. Dirty water can disturb the whole food chain.

examples: Small fish eaten by big fish

importantKeywords.definition: A series that shows how energy passes when one living thing eats another.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Algae → small fish → big fish.

importantKeywords.definition: A living thing like a plant that makes its own food.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Aquatic plants are producers.

importantKeywords.definition: A living thing that eats plants or animals.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A fish is a consumer.

importantKeywords.definition: An animal that hunts and eats another animal.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A bird catching fish is a predator.

importantKeywords.definition: A stable condition where living things support one another.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: A pond needs balance among plants and animals.

topics.quickSummary: A food chain shows the feeding relationships that keep a water habitat balanced.
revisionNotes: Food chains show who eats whom.
Plants and tiny organisms are often the beginning.
Animals depend on other living things for food.
Removing one animal can disturb the habitat.

topics.topic: Origin and Course of a River
topics.meaning: A river begins at a source, flows across land and finally meets another water body.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter follows the Godavari from its origin near Nashik to its delta near the sea, showing how a river travels through forests, towns, fields and plains.
topics.easyExplanation: A river is like a traveller. It begins at a high place, flows through many regions and reaches the sea.

stepByStepExplanation.title: A river usually begins in a high place
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Many rivers start from mountains, springs, glaciers or places where rainwater collects.

stepByStepExplanation.title: The river flows downward
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Because land slopes downward, river water moves from high land towards lower land.

stepByStepExplanation.title: The river receives more water
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Small streams and tributaries join the main river and increase its water.

stepByStepExplanation.title: The river supports places on the way
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: As it flows, the river gives water to forests, farms, villages, towns, animals and birds.

stepByStepExplanation.title: The river reaches a larger water body
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Many rivers finally meet a sea, ocean, lake or delta area. This complete path is called the river’s course.

topics.meaning: Godavari is called Dakshina Ganga because it is a sacred river flowing through southern India.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter connects the river with stories, culture, holy dips, Pushkaram and the lives of people along its banks.
topics.easyExplanation: A river is not only water; it is also connected with stories, faith and festivals.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Locate the river
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The Godavari is an important river of southern India and begins near Nashik in Maharashtra.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Understand the name Dakshina Ganga
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: It is called Dakshina Ganga because it is sacred and important to people in southern India, just as the Ganga is important in the north.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Follow its journey
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The river flows through different regions, receives tributaries, supports many settlements and finally reaches the sea.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Connect with culture
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: People have stories, festivals and religious practices connected with rivers like the Godavari.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Learn the responsibility
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A sacred and useful river must be kept clean because people, animals, plants and cultures depend on it.

topics.meaning: A river ecosystem includes water, plants, animals and people connected to the river.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter describes forests and wildlife around Godavari and explains that freshwater rivers support fish, turtles, birds and many other living things.
topics.easyExplanation: Rivers are homes for many living things and also support forests and people.

stepByStepExplanation.title: A river is more than flowing water
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A river includes water, plants, fish, insects, birds, animals, soil and people living near it.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Plants give shelter and food
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: River plants and nearby trees provide food, oxygen, shade and shelter for many organisms.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Aquatic animals live in the river
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Fish, turtles, frogs and other animals depend on clean water to breathe, move and find food.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Birds and people also depend on rivers
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Birds come to rivers for fish and water. People use rivers for drinking water, farming, travel and culture.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Pollution disturbs the ecosystem
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: When waste, chemicals or dirty water enter a river, many parts of the river ecosystem are harmed together.

importantKeywords.definition: A community of living things and non-living things interacting in one place.

topics.meaning: Rivers support drinking water, farming, transport, fishing, electricity and culture.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter shows that river water reaches houses, schools and fields and supports crops, fishers, forests and community life.
topics.easyExplanation: People use rivers for many needs, but they should not misuse or dirty them.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Drinking and daily needs
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: River water can be cleaned and used for drinking, cooking, washing and other daily work.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Farming
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Farmers use river water for irrigation so that crops can grow even when rainfall is not enough.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Transport and livelihood
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: In some places, rivers help boats move goods and people. Fishers and other workers also depend on rivers.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Culture and festivals
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Rivers are linked with stories, rituals, fairs and festivals in many parts of India.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use wisely
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Because many people share river water, we should not waste or pollute it.

importantKeywords.definition: Supplying water to crops through canals, pipes or other methods.

topics.meaning: A dam is a strong wall built across a river to store and control water.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter introduces dams such as Sriram Sagar Project and Polavaram Dam and explains that dams store water but also change river flow and affect nearby life.
topics.easyExplanation: Dams are like big water storage walls, but they must be planned carefully.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Understand why water is stored
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Rain does not fall equally every day, so people need ways to store water for later use.

stepByStepExplanation.title: A dam blocks and stores river water
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A dam is built across a river to hold water in a large reservoir.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Stored water helps people
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Dam water can be used for irrigation, drinking supply, electricity generation and flood control.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Dams also change river life
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: When a dam is built, the flow of water changes and some plants, animals and people may be affected.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use water thoughtfully
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Stored water should be used carefully because rivers are shared by many living beings and communities.

topics.meaning: River pollution happens when waste, chemicals or dirty water enter a river.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter asks students to think about waste thrown at home or school and what would happen if it entered rivers. It explains that plastics and harmful chemicals can hide in water and harm fish, birds and people.
topics.easyExplanation: A river becomes dirty when people throw waste into it. Dirty rivers harm animals and humans.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Identify what enters rivers
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Plastic, garbage, sewage, chemicals, soaps and fertilisers can enter rivers from homes, farms and factories.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Some pollution is visible
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Plastic bags, wrappers and floating waste can be seen easily on the surface of water.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Some pollution is hidden
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Chemicals and dirty soapy water may dissolve or mix in water, so the river can look clean but still be unsafe.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Pollution harms life
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Fish may not get enough oxygen, plants may grow too much, and people may get diseases from unsafe water.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Every small action helps
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Throw waste in bins, reduce plastic, keep drains clean and stop dirty water from reaching rivers.

importantKeywords.definition: Making air, water or land dirty and harmful by adding waste or harmful substances.

topics.meaning: A flood happens when too much water spreads over normally dry land.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter explains that heavy rain can make a river expand and flood nearby areas, and gives safety tips for before, during and after floods.
topics.easyExplanation: Too much rain can make rivers overflow. People must stay safe and follow instructions.

stepByStepExplanation.title: A flood happens when there is too much water
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Heavy rain or overflowing rivers can spread water over land that is usually dry.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Floods affect daily life
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Floods can damage homes, roads, crops, electricity, schools and drinking water sources.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Trees reduce flood impact
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Trees slow down rainwater and help soil absorb water. Without trees, water runs faster and can cause more flooding.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Safety before a flood
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Stay informed, prepare an emergency bag, know safe routes and move animals and people to safer places.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Safety during and after a flood
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Move to higher ground, avoid flood water, do not touch power lines, and return home only when it is declared safe.

importantKeywords.definition: A situation in which too much water covers land that is usually dry.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Heavy rain can cause a flood near a river.

topics.meaning: A river may run dry when there is too little rain or too much water is taken out.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter explains that water is limited and shared, and mentions efforts like the National Water Mission and careful water use.
topics.easyExplanation: If we waste water or take too much from rivers, less water remains for others.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Understand what a dry river means
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A river runs dry when water becomes very little or disappears from parts of its course.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Daily life becomes difficult
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Without water, people struggle to drink, cook, bathe, clean and grow crops.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Animals and plants suffer too
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Fish, birds, cattle, trees and crops depend on river water, so they are also affected.

stepByStepExplanation.title: People may have to move
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If there is no water for a long time, families may leave their homes and search for places with water.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Sharing water is responsibility
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Water should be used wisely, reused when possible, and shared fairly so that everyone gets enough.

topics.meaning: Food spoilage means food becoming unfit to eat, often because of microbes.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: Through Disha’s forgotten tiffin, the chapter explains how microbes in air, soil and water grow on food and spoil it when they get moisture, air and warmth.
topics.easyExplanation: Food can go bad if tiny microbes grow on it. Spoiled food may smell bad and make us sick.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Notice the spoiled food
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: When food smells bad, changes colour, becomes slimy or gets patches, it may be spoiled.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Understand microbes
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Tiny living things called microbes can grow on food. Some microbes spoil food and may make us sick.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Know what microbes need
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Microbes grow faster when they get moisture, air and a suitable warm temperature.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Connect with the forgotten tiffin
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The uttapam left for days in the lunch box had moisture and warmth, so mould grew on it.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Learn food safety
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Do not eat spoiled food. Store food properly, keep it covered and finish cooked food on time.

topics.meaning: Food preservation means keeping food safe for a longer time by slowing or stopping microbes.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter explores drying, refrigeration, airtight packing, adding salt, sugar or oil and other ways to preserve food.
topics.easyExplanation: Preservation saves food from spoiling quickly.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Understand the problem
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Food can spoil if microbes grow on it, especially when it has moisture and is kept warm for a long time.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Preservation slows microbes
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Food preservation means using methods that stop or slow the growth of microbes.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use salt, sugar, oil or spices
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Pickles, jams and some traditional foods last longer because these ingredients make it difficult for microbes to grow.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use drying and airtight storage
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Removing moisture and keeping food away from air helps food stay safe for a longer time.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Connect with daily life
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Aam papad, papad, pickles, dried fish, jams and packaged foods are examples of preserved food.

importantKeywords.definition: Keeping food cold to slow the growth of microbes and keep food fresh longer.

topics.meaning: Drying removes water from food so microbes cannot grow easily.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter shows chillies and tomato slices dried in sunlight to observe how removing moisture helps keep food longer.
topics.easyExplanation: Microbes need moisture. If food is dried, microbes grow slowly.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Microbes need moisture
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Many microbes need water to grow on food.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Drying removes water
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: When food is kept in sunlight or dried properly, moisture reduces from it.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Less moisture slows spoilage
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Without enough moisture, microbes cannot grow quickly, so food lasts longer.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Observe drying changes
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Tomato slices, papad or mango pulp become smaller, drier and lighter after drying in the Sun.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Store dry food properly
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: After drying, food should be kept clean and covered so moisture and dust do not enter again.

importantKeywords.definition: Removing moisture from food, often by keeping it in sunlight, to slow spoilage.

topics.meaning: Refrigeration means keeping food cold to slow down microbes.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter explains that cold temperature slows microbes, so milk, butter and many cooked foods last longer in a refrigerator.
topics.easyExplanation: Cold does not always kill microbes, but it slows their growth.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Warmth helps microbes grow
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Spoilage happens faster when food is kept in warm places for a long time.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Cold slows microbial growth
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A refrigerator keeps food cool. In cold conditions, many microbes grow slowly.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use refrigeration for short-term storage
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Cooked food, milk, curd, fruits and vegetables can stay fresh longer in a refrigerator.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Cold storage helps markets
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Fruits and vegetables may be kept in cold storage so they do not spoil quickly before reaching people.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Refrigeration is not magic
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Food still becomes bad after some time, so we should check smell, colour and freshness before eating.

topics.meaning: Fermentation is a process in which useful microbes change food and can make it soft, sour, fluffy or healthier.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter shows that microbes in air help idli batter rise and mentions fermented foods eaten in different parts of India.
topics.easyExplanation: Not all microbes are bad. Some help make food like idli, curd and fermented rice.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Not all microbes are harmful
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Some microbes are useful and help us make tasty and healthy foods.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Fermentation changes food
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: During fermentation, good microbes act on food and change its taste, texture and softness.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Idli batter becomes soft
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Microbes in the batter produce tiny gas bubbles, making idlis soft and fluffy.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Many Indian foods use fermentation
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Curd, idli, dosa batter, dhokla, kanji and fermented rice are examples.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use safe fermentation
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Clean vessels, correct time and good ingredients are important so useful microbes grow safely.

topics.meaning: Seasonal fruits are fruits that grow naturally in a particular season.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter’s vegetable market visit teaches that seasonal fruits taste best, are often fresher and connect us with local climate and farming.
topics.easyExplanation: Eating seasonal fruits is tasty and healthy because they are fresh.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Food changes with seasons
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Different fruits and vegetables grow better in different seasons because plants need suitable weather.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Local food is fresher
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Food grown nearby reaches us faster and often stays fresher than food brought from far away.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Seasonal food supports health
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Seasonal foods often match what the body needs in that weather, such as cooling foods in summer.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Markets show seasonal change
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Vegetable markets have different fruits and vegetables in summer, monsoon and winter.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Respect local food habits
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Families and regions have traditional food practices that are linked with seasons, farming and health.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Food enters the mouth first
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Digestion begins when we put food in our mouth.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Different teeth do different work
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Incisors cut, canines tear, premolars crush and molars grind food.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Chewing breaks food into small pieces
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Small pieces are easier to swallow and easier for the body to digest.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Saliva mixes with food
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Saliva makes food soft and starts helping in digestion.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Chew slowly and safely
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Eating slowly, chewing well and not talking while eating helps prevent choking and supports digestion.

topics.meaning: Food safety includes eating clean food, protecting teeth and avoiding choking hazards.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter discusses cavities, proper chewing, safe eating, and the danger of choking when food blocks the airway.
topics.easyExplanation: Clean teeth, careful chewing and safe eating habits protect health.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Safe food protects health
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Food should be clean, fresh and properly stored so that it does not cause illness.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Cavities form when teeth are not cleaned
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Food particles and germs can damage teeth and create holes called cavities.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Brush and rinse regularly
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Brushing twice a day and rinsing after meals helps keep teeth healthy.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Choking happens when food blocks breathing
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If someone swallows large pieces quickly or talks while eating, food can enter the wrong path.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Eat carefully
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Sit properly, chew well, avoid rushing and take small bites to reduce choking risk.

importantKeywords.definition: A small hole in a tooth caused by germs and leftover food particles damaging the tooth.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Improve after observing
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The purpose is not only to find problems but also to suggest actions like fixing leaks, planting trees and saving electricity.

topics.meaning: separating waste into different types so it can be reused, recycled or composted
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter uses a segregation game with boxes for wet waste, dry waste and harmful waste. It teaches correct waste management.
topics.easyExplanation: Do not mix all waste. Separate it so it can be handled safely.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Waste is not all the same
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Different types of waste need different treatment. Mixing everything makes cleaning and recycling difficult.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Separate wet waste
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Food leftovers, vegetable peels and leaves are wet or biodegradable waste and can become compost.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Separate dry waste
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Paper, plastic, metal and glass are dry waste. Some of these can be reused or recycled.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Use different bins
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Putting waste into correct bins keeps the school clean and helps workers handle waste safely.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Segregation reduces pollution
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: When waste is sorted, less garbage goes to open areas, drains or water bodies.

topics.meaning: turning wet organic waste into useful manure for plants

topics.easyExplanation: Composting changes waste like leaves and peels into food for soil.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Compost begins with natural waste
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Vegetable peels, fruit skins, dry leaves and garden waste can be used for composting.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Microbes break waste down
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Tiny living things slowly decompose this waste and turn it into dark, soil-like manure.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Compost needs air and moisture
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The waste should be kept slightly moist and turned sometimes so that air reaches it.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Compost feeds plants
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The final compost adds nutrients to soil and helps plants grow naturally.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Composting reduces garbage
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Instead of throwing all wet waste into dustbins, composting changes it into something useful.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Water is used in drinking areas, toilets, handwashing, cleaning, gardening and sometimes kitchens.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Find wastage points
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Running taps, leaking pipes, overflowing tanks and careless use waste water.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Take simple actions
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Close taps, report leaks, use only needed water and avoid playing with drinking water.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Reuse where safe
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Water left after washing vegetables or suitable grey water can sometimes be used for plants with adult guidance.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Make saving a habit
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: When students save water at school, they learn to save it at home and in the community too.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Green spaces support life

stepByStepExplanation.title: Planting is long-term care

stepByStepExplanation.title: Safety is everyone's duty

stepByStepExplanation.title: A kind school feels happy

stepByStepExplanation.title: Understand the Ashoka Chakra
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The navy-blue wheel in the centre shows movement, law, progress and doing our duty.

stepByStepExplanation.explanation: A person from Rajasthan, Kerala, Assam or Kashmir may eat different food, wear different clothes and celebrate different festivals.

topics.topic: Silk and Life Cycle of Silkworm
topics.meaning: Silk is a soft, shiny natural fibre obtained from the cocoon made by silkworms.
topics.ncertBasedExplanation: The chapter explains clothes and fibres and introduces silk as a natural fibre. Silk comes from the cocoon of a silkworm. The silkworm grows through stages: egg, caterpillar or silkworm, cocoon or pupa, and adult moth. This helps students understand that some clothes come from plants, some from animals, and some from human-made materials.
topics.easyExplanation: Silk cloth comes from a tiny living creature called a silkworm. The silkworm eats mulberry leaves, grows bigger, makes a cocoon around itself using silk thread, and this thread is used to make silk yarn and silk cloth.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Silk comes from a living creature
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Silk is a natural fibre. It is obtained from the cocoon made by silkworms. A silkworm is the caterpillar stage of a silk moth.

stepByStepExplanation.title: The silk moth lays eggs
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The life cycle begins when an adult female silk moth lays tiny eggs.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Eggs hatch into silkworms
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: Small caterpillars come out of the eggs. These caterpillars are called silkworms and they mainly eat mulberry leaves.

stepByStepExplanation.title: The silkworm makes a cocoon
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: After growing, the silkworm produces a long sticky silk thread and wraps it around itself to make a cocoon.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Silk thread is taken from the cocoon
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: People collect cocoons and carefully take out silk fibres. These fibres are spun into silk yarn.

stepByStepExplanation.title: Silk yarn becomes cloth
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: The yarn is woven into silk cloth, which is used for sarees, scarves and other clothes.

stepByStepExplanation.title: The life cycle can continue
stepByStepExplanation.explanation: If the cocoon is allowed to develop naturally, the pupa changes into a moth. The moth lays eggs and the cycle starts again.

importantKeywords.definition: The caterpillar stage of a silk moth; it eats mulberry leaves and makes a silk cocoon.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Silkworms eat mulberry leaves and make cocoons.

importantKeywords.definition: A protective covering made by a silkworm using silk thread around its body.
importantKeywords.simpleExample: Silk thread is obtained from a cocoon.

importantKeywords.definition: A soft, shiny natural fibre obtained from the cocoon of silkworms.
